name: Dessie Woods
description: Black woman from Georgia who in 1975 shot and killed a white insurance agent who attempted to rape her and her companion Cheryl Todd. Sentenced to 22 years in prison and became the focus of a major national defense campaign through the late 1970s, including by PFOC. Released in 1981. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder (shot would-be rapist in self-defense)

name: Eugene Graham
description: One of the 'San Quentin Two,' convicted along with Eugene Allen of killing a guard at the Deuel Vocational Institution in 1973 and sentenced to death. Defense effort framed the case as a frame-up of Black activist prisoners. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder of a prison guard at Deuel Vocational Institution

name: Eugene Allen
description: One of the 'San Quentin Two,' convicted with Eugene 'Ernest' Graham of killing a guard at the Deuel Vocational Institution in 1973 and sentenced to death. Their defense campaign argued the men were targeted because of their organizing inside California prisons. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder of a prison guard at Deuel Vocational Institution

name: Safiya Bukhari
description: Black Liberation Army member and former Black Panther who was briefly liberated from the Tombs in New York during a 1976 escape, recaptured, and held in federal and New York state custody. Cofounder of the Jericho Movement for political prisoners. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Bank robbery, weapons charges

name: Henry Brown
description: Black Liberation Army member who was briefly liberated from the Manhattan House of Detention (the Tombs) in 1976, recaptured, and eventually held at FCI Lewisburg as a North American political prisoner. Listed in PFOC Breakthrough magazine through the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Bank expropriation, weapons charges, escape from the Tombs

name: Gabriel Torres
description: New Afrikan political prisoner associated with the Black Liberation Army, listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s. Held in U.S. state custody on charges arising from clandestine BLA activity. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black Liberation Army activity

name: Cisco Torres
description: Black Liberation Army-associated political prisoner; later one of the San Francisco 8 charged in 2007 for the 1971 killing of a San Francisco police officer. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s while held on BLA-related charges. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black Liberation Army activity; later San Francisco 8 case

name: Charles Jennings
description: One of the 'Stateville Four,' Black prisoners at Stateville Correctional Center in Illinois framed for the 1977 stabbing of a prison guard following organizing inside the prison. Their case was a focus of PFOC and other support campaigns. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder of prison guard (framed)

name: Thomas Blair
description: One of the 'Stateville Four,' Black prisoners at Stateville Correctional Center in Illinois framed for the 1977 stabbing of a prison guard after organizing inside the prison. Defense campaign supported by PFOC. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder of prison guard (framed)

name: Andrew Craig
description: One of the 'Stateville Four,' Black prisoners at Stateville Correctional Center in Illinois framed for the 1977 stabbing of a prison guard. Held up by PFOC and others as a clear case of retaliation against politicized prisoners. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder of prison guard (framed)

name: John Myles
description: One of the 'Stateville Four,' Black prisoners at Stateville Correctional Center in Illinois framed for the 1977 killing of a prison guard. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder of prison guard (framed)

name: Ahmed Evans
description: Black nationalist leader convicted of first-degree murder for the July 1968 Glenville shootout in Cleveland with police, in which three officers and four others were killed. Sentenced to death; sentence commuted to life after Ohio's death penalty was struck down. Died in prison in 1978. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: First-degree murder (Glenville shootout)

name: Lance Bell
description: New Afrikan political prisoner listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s, held in U.S. custody on charges arising from Black liberation activity. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: George Merritt
description: Black activist convicted in 1967 in connection with the death of a white truck driver killed during the Plainfield, New Jersey rebellion. Spent more than a decade in prison through repeated trials and reversals; his case was a long-running PFOC and Black liberation defense campaign. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder (Plainfield rebellion, 1967)

name: John Hill
description: Mohawk and Black activist, also known as Dacajeweiah ('Splitting the Sky'), convicted in connection with a guard's death during the 1971 Attica Prison uprising and sentenced to 20 years to life at Sing Sing. The Attica Brothers defense was a major focus of PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder of a guard during the Attica uprising

name: Jomo Joka Omowale
description: Attica Brother charged in connection with the 1971 Attica Prison uprising and the death of a guard. His prosecution as part of the Attica Brothers case was a focal point of post-Attica political prisoner defense work, including in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges arising from the 1971 Attica uprising

name: Addis Ababa
description: Citizen of the Republic of New Afrika and one of the 'RNA-11,' arrested in the August 1971 Jackson, Mississippi police and FBI raid on the RNA Provisional Government house, in which a Jackson police officer was killed. Convicted and imprisoned on charges arising from the raid. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder, assault and treason against the state of Mississippi (RNA-11 case)

name: Karim Njabafundi
description: Citizen of the Republic of New Afrika and one of the 'RNA-11,' arrested in the August 1971 FBI/police raid on the RNA Provisional Government house in Jackson, Mississippi. Convicted and imprisoned on charges arising from the raid. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder, assault and treason against the state of Mississippi (RNA-11 case)

name: Offogga Quddus
description: Citizen of the Republic of New Afrika and one of the 'RNA-11,' arrested in the August 18, 1971 FBI/police raid on the RNA Provisional Government house in Jackson, Mississippi. Convicted on charges arising from the raid. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder, assault and treason against the state of Mississippi (RNA-11 case)

name: Tamu Sana
description: Citizen of the Republic of New Afrika and one of the 'RNA-11,' arrested in the August 18, 1971 FBI/police raid on the RNA Provisional Government house in Jackson, Mississippi. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges arising from the RNA-11 raid

name: Njeri Quddus
description: Citizen of the Republic of New Afrika and one of the 'RNA-11,' arrested in the August 18, 1971 FBI/police raid on the RNA Provisional Government house in Jackson, Mississippi. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges arising from the RNA-11 raid

name: Chuma
description: Citizen of the Republic of New Afrika and one of the 'RNA-11,' arrested in the August 18, 1971 FBI/police raid on the RNA Provisional Government house in Jackson, Mississippi. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges arising from the RNA-11 raid

name: Solomon Brown
description: Black Liberation Army member arrested in connection with the October 20, 1981 attempted Brink's armored car expropriation in Nyack, New York, in which a guard and two police officers were killed. Convicted on related charges and listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to the 1981 Brink's/Nyack action

name: Cecilio Ferguson
description: Black Liberation Army member and Republic of New Afrika citizen, arrested March 22, 1982 and charged in the Brink's/Nyack RICO conspiracy. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine from the mid-1980s through 1990s while held at FCI Lewisburg. BOP #04372-054. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: RICO conspiracy related to the 1981 Brink's/Nyack action

name: Eddie Josephs
description: Former Black Panther and Black Liberation Army associate, charged in the Brink's/Nyack RICO conspiracy in the early 1980s. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: RICO conspiracy related to the 1981 Brink's/Nyack action

name: Nehanda Abiodun
description: New Afrikan revolutionary and BLA-associated activist named in the Brink's/Nyack RICO indictment in the early 1980s. She went underground and later lived in exile in Cuba until her death in 2019. Listed as a North American political prisoner/fugitive in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: RICO conspiracy related to the 1981 Brink's/Nyack action

name: Bilal Sunni-Ali
description: New Afrikan activist and musician indicted in the Brink's/Nyack RICO conspiracy case in the early 1980s. Acquitted at trial. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine while held pretrial. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: RICO conspiracy related to the 1981 Brink's/Nyack action

name: Iliana Robinson
description: Defendant in the Brink's/Nyack RICO indictment in the early 1980s, listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine while in pretrial federal custody. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: RICO conspiracy related to the 1981 Brink's/Nyack action

name: Nilsa Cobeo
description: Defendant in the Brink's/Nyack RICO indictment in the early 1980s, held in federal pretrial detention and listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: RICO conspiracy related to the 1981 Brink's/Nyack action

name: Larry Mack
description: Black Liberation Army member held at the Metropolitan Correctional Center in New York in connection with BLA-related federal charges in the early 1980s. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to Black Liberation Army activity

name: Heshimu Lloyd Brice
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held in Santa Clara County, California, listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Kalima Aswad
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held at the California Men's Colony in San Luis Obispo, CDC #B24120. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Ashanti Alston
description: Former Black Panther and Black Liberation Army member who served more than a decade in federal and Connecticut state prisons, including at Somers, on charges arising from BLA activity. Later became a prominent anarchist organizer and writer. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Bank expropriation and weapons charges related to BLA activity

name: Haki Malik Abdullah
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held in California prisons including Folsom and Corcoran, CDC #C-56123. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine throughout the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Awali Stoneham
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held in California prisons including Soledad and Folsom, CDC #B-98168. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Richard Thompson-El
description: New Afrikan/Moorish political prisoner held at USP Marion and later Stillwater Prison in Minnesota, #155229. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Charles Scott
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held at San Quentin State Prison in California, listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Mohaman Geuka Koti
description: New Afrikan elder political prisoner held at Great Meadow Correctional Facility in New York, NYSDOCS #80-A-808. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine through the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Teddy Heath
description: Black Liberation Army member held at Attica Correctional Facility, NYSDOCS #75-A-0139, on charges arising from BLA activity in the 1970s. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Died in custody. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black Liberation Army activity

name: Kazi Toure
description: Member of the United Freedom Front, convicted in the mid-1980s of seditious conspiracy and RICO charges arising from a string of anti-imperialist bombings. Held at MCI Walpole in Massachusetts and later FCI Lewisburg. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, RICO (United Freedom Front case)

name: Carol Hill
description: Black activist held at the Metropolitan Correctional Center in New York in connection with BLA-related federal charges in the early 1980s. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to Black Liberation Army activity

name: Shaheem Jabbar
description: New Afrikan grand jury resister jailed for contempt during 1980s federal grand jury investigations into the Black Liberation Army and the Republic of New Afrika. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt for refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury

name: Yaasmyn Fula
description: New Afrikan grand jury resister jailed for civil contempt during 1980s federal grand jury investigations into the Black Liberation Army and the Republic of New Afrika. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt for refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury

name: Eve Rosahn
description: White anti-imperialist activist jailed for civil contempt in the early 1980s after refusing to cooperate with federal grand juries investigating the Black Liberation Army and the 1981 Brink's/Nyack case. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt for refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury

name: Fulani Sunni-Ali
description: Republic of New Afrika minister and chairperson, jailed for civil contempt in the early 1980s after refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury investigating the Black Liberation Army and the Brink's/Nyack case. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt for refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury

name: Asha Sundiata
description: New Afrikan grand jury resister jailed for civil contempt during 1980s federal grand jury investigations into the Black Liberation Army and the Republic of New Afrika. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt for refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury

name: Jerry Gaines
description: Republic of New Afrika citizen jailed for civil contempt in the 1980s after refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury investigating the Black Liberation Army and related activity. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt for refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury

name: Colette Pean
description: Member of the 'New York 8' (sometimes the 'New York 8+'), a group of New Afrikan activists arrested October 18, 1984 in coordinated FBI/NYPD raids and charged with conspiracy to commit armed expropriations and prison breaks. The case largely collapsed at trial. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy charges (New York 8 case)

name: Bashir Hameed
description: Former Black Panther convicted with Abdul Majid (the 'NY Two') in the 1981 killing of an NYPD officer, after multiple trials. Held at Great Meadow Correctional Facility in New York, NYSDOCS #82-A-6313. Died in prison in 2008. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder of an NYPD officer (NY Two case)

name: Larry Guy
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held at Jackson State Prison in Michigan, listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Watani Tyehimba
description: Founder of the New Afrikan People's Organization (NAPO) jailed for 18 months for civil contempt after refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury investigating the Black Liberation Army and the Republic of New Afrika. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt for refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury

name: Tariq James Haskins
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held in federal custody, BOP #40075-133, listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Martin Rutrell
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held at the Raiford prison complex in Florida, #042600. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Sababu Na Uhuru
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held in federal custody, BOP #07350-016, listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Rickke Green
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held at the Oklahoma State Penitentiary, #84244. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Ahmad Abdur Rahman
description: Former Black Panther convicted in a 1971 Detroit killing and sentenced to life in Michigan, #130539. Spent more than 20 years in prison, including at Jackson, before his sentence was commuted; later became a professor of history. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder (Detroit, 1971)

name: William Allen
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held in Tennessee state custody, #66843, listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Ojore Lutalo
description: New Afrikan anarchist and former Black Liberation Army member, convicted in 1975 of an attempted bank expropriation and armed self-defense against police, and held for decades in the Management Control Unit at Trenton State Prison in New Jersey. Released in 2009. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine throughout the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Bank expropriation, armed self-defense against police

name: Lorenzo Stone-Bey
description: New Afrikan/Moorish political prisoner held at the Indiana State Prison, #10006, who became a recognized writer and organizer inside. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Aida Robinson
description: New Afrikan woman political prisoner held in federal custody at FCI Dublin in California, BOP #03323-097. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Isma-El Rahim
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held at Auburn Correctional Facility in New York. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Thomas Warner
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held at SCI Huntingdon in Pennsylvania, #M3049. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Masai Ehehosi
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held at Lawrenceville Correctional Center in Virginia, captured in 1985 with Sekou Odinga / RNA associates. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Abdul Haqq
description: New Afrikan political prisoner first held at Rikers Island and later in New York State custody under NYSDOCS #89-T-1710. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the late 1980s and 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Herman Ferguson
description: Longtime Black nationalist organizer and associate of Malcolm X, convicted in 1968 of conspiracy to murder civil rights leaders, fled into exile, and returned in 1989 to serve his sentence at Attica Correctional Facility, NYSDOCS #89-A-4621. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit murder (1968 frame-up)

name: James Miller
description: New Afrikan political prisoner held at the U.S. Penitentiary at Marion, Illinois, listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine during the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to Black liberation activity

name: Kuwasi Balagoon
description: New Afrikan anarchist, former Black Panther, and Black Liberation Army soldier convicted in the 1981 Brink's/Nyack expropriation. Died of AIDS-related illness in Auburn Correctional Facility in December 1986. Listed as a North American political prisoner in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Murder, robbery (1981 Brink's/Nyack action)

name: Carlos Perez Africa
description: MOVE 9 member convicted in the August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation that killed Philadelphia police officer James Ramp. Sentenced to 30-100 years and held for decades in Pennsylvania DOC custody. Released on parole in the late 2010s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Third-degree murder, conspiracy, assault — August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation

name: Michael Davis Africa
description: MOVE 9 member (also known as Michael Hill Africa) convicted in the August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation that killed Philadelphia police officer James Ramp. Brother of Mike Africa Sr. (a separate MOVE 9 member). Sentenced to 30-100 years in Pennsylvania DOC custody. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Third-degree murder, conspiracy, assault — August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation

name: Edward Goodman Africa
description: MOVE 9 member convicted in the August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation that killed Philadelphia police officer James Ramp. Sentenced to 30-100 years and held for decades in Pennsylvania DOC custody. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Third-degree murder, conspiracy, assault — August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation

name: Consusuella Dotson Africa
description: MOVE 9 member convicted in the August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation that killed Philadelphia police officer James Ramp. Sentenced to 30-100 years and held in Pennsylvania DOC custody, including at SCI Muncy. Died in prison in 1998. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Third-degree murder, conspiracy, assault — August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation

name: Sue Leon Africa
description: MOVE 9 member (also known as Sue Savino Africa) convicted in the August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation that killed Philadelphia police officer James Ramp. Sentenced to 30-100 years and held in Pennsylvania DOC custody at SCI Muncy. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Third-degree murder, conspiracy, assault — August 8, 1978 Powelton Village confrontation

name: Alberta Wicker Africa
description: MOVE family member imprisoned in Pennsylvania DOC custody on charges arising from confrontations between MOVE and Philadelphia authorities. Held in the same period as the MOVE 9 and central to MOVE's organizing for political prisoner support. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Charges arising from MOVE confrontations with Philadelphia authorities

name: Raphael Kwesi Joseph
description: One of the Virgin Islands 5, convicted in the 1972 Fountain Valley golf course killings on St. Croix that left eight people dead. Held in US federal custody including at USP Lompoc; case was framed by supporters as a colonial prosecution of Black liberation activists. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: First-degree murder, robbery — Fountain Valley, St. Croix, September 6, 1972

name: Abdul Aziz
description: One of the Virgin Islands 5, convicted in the 1972 Fountain Valley golf course killings on St. Croix. Held in US federal custody including at USP Leavenworth. Supporters argued the prosecution targeted Black liberation activists and that confessions were extracted under torture. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: First-degree murder, robbery — Fountain Valley, St. Croix, September 6, 1972

name: Malik El-Amin
description: One of the Virgin Islands 5 (born Meral Smith), convicted in the 1972 Fountain Valley golf course killings on St. Croix. Held in US federal custody on eight consecutive life sentences. Supporters argued the prosecution targeted Black liberation activists in the colonized Virgin Islands. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: First-degree murder, robbery — Fountain Valley, St. Croix, September 6, 1972

name: Clayton Van Lydegraf
description: Longtime white communist and anti-imperialist organizer, formerly of the CPUSA and later associated with the Weather Underground and Prairie Fire Organizing Committee. Arrested in November 1977 in Los Angeles as one of the PFOC/LA 5 on conspiracy and explosives charges tied to plans to bomb the office of California State Senator John Briggs. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, possession of explosives — LA 5 case

name: Judith Bissell
description: White anti-imperialist activist arrested in November 1977 in Los Angeles as one of the PFOC/LA 5 on conspiracy and explosives charges tied to a plan to bomb California State Senator John Briggs's office. Spouse of Weather Underground member Silas Trim Bissell. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, possession of explosives — LA 5 case

name: Leslie Mullin
description: White anti-imperialist activist arrested in November 1977 in Los Angeles as one of the PFOC/LA 5 on conspiracy and explosives charges; booked under the alias Grace Fortmer. Continued solidarity work after release. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, possession of explosives — LA 5 case

name: Marc Perry
description: White anti-imperialist activist arrested in November 1977 in Los Angeles as one of the PFOC/LA 5 on conspiracy and explosives charges tied to a plan to bomb the office of California State Senator John Briggs. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, possession of explosives — LA 5 case

name: Michael Justesen
description: White anti-imperialist activist and former Weather Underground member arrested in November 1977 in Los Angeles as one of the PFOC/LA 5 on conspiracy and explosives charges. Had previously lived underground after Weather Underground activity in the early 1970s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, possession of explosives — LA 5 case

name: Silas Trim Bissell
description: White anti-imperialist organizer and Weather Underground fugitive who lived under an assumed identity for 17 years after a 1970 ROTC bombing attempt at the University of Washington. Captured in Eugene, Oregon in January 1987 and prosecuted on federal explosives and fugitive charges. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Attempted bombing of ROTC building, University of Washington; unlawful flight

name: Susan Saxe
description: Brandeis student and anti-war militant who participated in the September 23, 1970 robbery of a Brighton, Massachusetts bank to fund anti-Vietnam War activity, during which Boston police officer Walter Schroeder was killed. Lived underground until her 1975 arrest and was sentenced to 12 years. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Bank robbery, manslaughter — September 23, 1970 Brighton, MA bank robbery

name: Therese Coupez
description: White anti-imperialist activist imprisoned in federal custody at FCI Pleasanton on resistance-related charges in the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine as part of the network of North American anti-imperialist political prisoners.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to anti-imperialist resistance activity

name: Steven Burke
description: White anti-imperialist activist imprisoned at FCI Lewisburg in the 1980s on charges related to resistance and solidarity activity. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to anti-imperialist resistance activity

name: Andrew Magers
description: White anti-imperialist activist imprisoned at FCI Lewisburg in the 1980s on charges related to anti-imperialist resistance work. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to anti-imperialist resistance activity

name: Shelley Miller
description: White anti-imperialist activist imprisoned at the Federal Prison Camp at Alderson, West Virginia in the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine as part of the network of North American anti-imperialist political prisoners.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to anti-imperialist resistance activity

name: Julie Nalibov
description: White anti-imperialist activist held in the High Security Unit (HSU) at FCI Lexington in the late 1980s, a small experimental control unit for women political prisoners that was eventually shut down after international human rights protest. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to anti-imperialist resistance activity

name: Christine Rico
description: White anti-imperialist activist held in the High Security Unit (HSU) at FCI Lexington in the late 1980s, the experimental women's control unit later shut down after human rights litigation. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to anti-imperialist resistance activity

name: Sandra Roland
description: White anti-imperialist activist held in the High Security Unit (HSU) at FCI Lexington in the late 1980s, an experimental women's control unit later closed after international human rights protest. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges related to anti-imperialist resistance activity

name: Claude Marks
description: White anti-imperialist activist who lived underground for nearly a decade before surrendering in 1994 with co-defendant Donna Willmott on charges related to a 1985 conspiracy to free Puerto Rican political prisoner Oscar López Rivera. Held at MCC Chicago and later released; co-founded the Freedom Archives in San Francisco. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.
affiliation: Confederated Tribes of the Colville Reservation

cases.charges: Second-degree murder, first-degree assault

cases.charges: Conspiracy / aiding fugitive (FBI agent killings, Pine Ridge)

cases.charges: First-degree murder of two FBI agents

cases.charges: First-degree murder of two FBI agents

cases.sentence: Cumulative federal time across multiple sentences

name: Bonnie Urfer
description: Longtime Nukewatch organizer and anti-nuclear activist arrested repeatedly for nonviolent direct action against the Navy's Project ELF extremely-low-frequency submarine transmitter in northern Wisconsin and Michigan. Convicted multiple times of federal trespass, accumulating years of jail time. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal trespass and related — multiple Project ELF transmitter protests
cases.sentence: Cumulative federal time across multiple sentences

name: Cory Bartholomew
description: Anti-nuclear activist arrested for nonviolent direct action against the Navy's Project ELF submarine transmitter and held at the Bayfield County Jail in Wisconsin. Convicted of federal trespass and related charges. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal trespass — Project ELF anti-nuclear protest

name: Maria Cueto
description: Chicana activist and former executive director of the Episcopal Church's National Commission on Hispanic Affairs, jailed in 1977 for refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury investigating the FALN (Fuerzas Armadas de Liberación Nacional). She served roughly a year on civil contempt charges and was later re-subpoenaed and jailed again. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.
affiliation: Episcopal Church National Commission on Hispanic Affairs

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury investigating FALN

name: Raisa Nemikin
description: Staff member of the Episcopal Church's National Commission on Hispanic Affairs jailed in 1977 alongside Maria Cueto for refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating the FALN. Her imprisonment became a major civil-liberties cause within the Episcopal Church and the Puerto Rican independence movement. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.
affiliation: Episcopal Church National Commission on Hispanic Affairs

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury investigating FALN

name: José López
description: Chicago-based leader of the Movimiento de Liberación Nacional Puertorriqueño (MLN) and longtime director of the Puerto Rican Cultural Center in Humboldt Park; brother of Oscar López Rivera. Jailed for civil contempt for refusing to cooperate with federal grand juries investigating the FALN in the late 1970s and 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury investigating FALN

name: Roberto Caldero
description: Chicago Puerto Rican community organizer and MLN activist jailed for civil contempt after refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating the FALN. His resistance was part of a broad campaign of non-collaboration by independentistas in the early 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury investigating FALN

name: Ricardo Romero
description: Chicano/Mexicano organizer with the MLN (Movimiento de Liberación Nacional Mexicano) jailed for civil contempt for refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating the FALN and Puerto Rican independence movement. His imprisonment helped link the Chicano-Mexicano and Puerto Rican liberation movements in the early 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury

name: Pedro Archuleta
description: New Mexico-based Chicano/Mexicano activist jailed for civil contempt after refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating the FALN and Puerto Rican independence movement. He was held alongside Ricardo Romero as part of the 1984-85 grand jury resistance wave. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury

name: Julio Rosado
description: New York-based MLN activist and one of three Rosado brothers jailed for civil contempt for refusing to testify before federal grand juries investigating the FALN. He was held at FCI Ashland and FCI Raybrook during the mid-1980s grand jury resistance campaign. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury investigating FALN

name: Andrés Rosado
description: New York-based MLN activist and brother of Julio and Luis Rosado, jailed for civil contempt after refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury investigating the FALN. He was held at FCI Texarkana and FCI Allenwood in the mid-1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury investigating FALN

name: Luis Rosado
description: New York-based MLN activist and brother of Julio and Andrés Rosado, jailed for civil contempt for refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating the FALN. The Rosado brothers' joint resistance became emblematic of the Puerto Rican independence movement's non-collaboration stance. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury investigating FALN

name: Federico Cintrón-Fiallo
description: Puerto Rican independentista jailed on civil contempt charges for refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating the clandestine independence movement. His resistance was widely supported by the Puerto Rican left and human rights organizations. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury

name: Carlos Noya-Muratti
description: Member of the Liga Socialista Puertorriqueña (LSP) jailed for 17 months on civil contempt charges for refusing to cooperate with a federal grand jury investigating the Puerto Rican independence movement. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury

name: Carlos Rosario Pantojas
description: Puerto Rican independentista jailed on civil contempt charges for refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating the clandestine independence movement in the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury

name: Ricarte Montes García
description: Member of the Liga Socialista Puertorriqueña (LSP) jailed for civil contempt after refusing to testify before a federal grand jury in New York investigating Puerto Rican independence organizations. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury

name: Nelson Ramirez
description: Puerto Rican independentista jailed on civil contempt charges for refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating the independence movement in the 1980s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury

name: William Morales
description: FALN member severely injured in a 1978 premature bomb explosion at a Queens, NY safehouse that took both of his hands and most of his face. Convicted on federal weapons and explosives charges, he made a dramatic escape from Bellevue Hospital prison ward in 1979, was recaptured in Mexico in 1983, and was eventually released to Cuba where he remains in exile. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal weapons and explosives charges; escape; later Mexican charges
cases.sentence: 29 years federal (escaped 1979; later freed to Cuba 1988)

name: Angel Rodríguez Cristóbal
description: Member of the Liga Socialista Puertorriqueña and one of the Vieques 4, arrested for protesting US Navy bombing on the island of Vieques. He was found hanged in his cell at FCI Tallahassee on November 11, 1979 under circumstances his comrades and family always maintained were a political killing rather than suicide. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Trespass on federal property — Vieques Navy bombing range protest

name: Ismael Guadalupe
description: Vieques community leader and one of the Vieques 4, sentenced in 1979 for trespassing on the US Navy bombing range in protest of the Navy's occupation of the island. He spent decades organizing against the Navy presence on Vieques. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.
affiliation: Crusade for the Rescue of Vieques

cases.charges: Trespass on federal property — Vieques Navy bombing range protest

name: Ramón Alicea Flores
description: One of the Vieques 4, sentenced in 1979 to federal prison for protesting US Navy bombing on Vieques. He served his sentence alongside Angel Rodríguez Cristóbal, whose death in custody became a major rallying point for the independence movement. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Trespass on federal property — Vieques Navy bombing range protest

name: Diego Ledée
description: One of the Vieques 4, sentenced in 1979 to federal prison for protesting US Navy bombing on the island of Vieques. The four were prosecuted to discourage further civil disobedience against the Navy's occupation. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Trespass on federal property — Vieques Navy bombing range protest

name: Nydia Esther Cuevas
description: Puerto Rican independentista who, with Pablo Marcano García, occupied the Chilean Consulate in San Juan in July 1978 to protest the imprisonment of the Nationalist prisoners and to highlight US imperialism in Latin America. She was convicted and imprisoned for the action. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Hostage-taking and kidnapping — Chilean Consulate occupation, San Juan

name: Pablo Marcano García
description: Puerto Rican independentista and artist who, with Nydia Cuevas, occupied the Chilean Consulate in San Juan on July 25, 1978 to protest the continued imprisonment of the Puerto Rican Nationalist prisoners and US-backed dictatorships in Latin America. He was convicted and served federal time. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Hostage-taking and kidnapping — Chilean Consulate occupation, San Juan

name: Steven Guerra
description: Puerto Rican independence supporter jailed for civil contempt for refusing to testify before a federal grand jury investigating the FALN. He was held at FCI Raybrook and FCI La Tuna during the 1980s grand jury resistance campaigns. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Civil contempt — refusal to testify before federal grand jury investigating FALN

name: Julio Veras
description: Puerto Rican political prisoner held at FCI Lewisburg and FCI Petersburg in the 1980s on charges connected to the independence movement. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Federal charges connected to Puerto Rican independence activity

name: Félix Rosa
description: Puerto Rican political prisoner held at Graham and Logan Correctional Centers in Illinois in connection with the independence movement and the FALN clandestine activity in Chicago. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.institution_name: Graham Correctional Center / Logan Correctional Center
cases.charges: State charges connected to Puerto Rican independence activity

name: Juan Segarra-Palmer
description: Macheteros (Ejército Popular Boricua) leader convicted in the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation, in which $7.2 million was taken from a Wells Fargo depot in West Hartford, Connecticut. He received a 65-year sentence and was the last of the Hartford defendants to be released, freed under President Clinton's 1999 commutation. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. BOP #15357-077.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and armed robbery — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Luz María Berríos Berríos
description: Macheteros member arrested in the 1985 federal sweep connected to the Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. She was held at FCI Lexington, including in the controversial high-security control unit for women. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. BOP #24582-004.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Ivonne Meléndez Carrión
description: Macheteros member arrested in the August 1985 federal sweep targeting the Ejército Popular Boricua in connection with the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. She was among the Hartford defendants prosecuted in Connecticut federal court. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Elías Samuel Castro
description: Macheteros member arrested in the August 1985 federal sweep connected to the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. He was one of the Hartford defendants prosecuted in US federal court for activities of the Ejército Popular Boricua. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Hilton Fernández Diamante
description: Macheteros member arrested in the August 1985 federal sweep targeting the Ejército Popular Boricua in connection with the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. He was held at FCI Englewood. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Orlando González-Claudio
description: Macheteros member arrested in the August 1985 federal sweep connected to the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. He was held at FCI Sheridan. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. BOP #03173-069.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Antonio Camacho Negrón
description: Macheteros member arrested in 1985 in connection with the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. After serving his initial federal sentence, he refused to comply with supervised release conditions as a matter of principle and was re-imprisoned in the 1990s. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. BOP #03587-069.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Luis Alfredo Colón Osorio
description: Macheteros member who went underground after the 1985 federal sweep targeting the Ejército Popular Boricua and was captured in 1992. He was prosecuted on federal charges connected to the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. BOP #03172-069.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Ángel Díaz Ruiz
description: Macheteros member arrested in the August 1985 federal sweep connected to the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. He was among the Hartford defendants prosecuted on conspiracy charges. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Roberto José Maldonado
description: Macheteros member arrested in the August 1985 federal sweep connected to the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. He was held at FMC Fort Worth. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. BOP #03588-069.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Norman Ramírez Talavera
description: Macheteros member arrested in the August 1985 federal sweep connected to the 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation. He was among the Hartford defendants prosecuted in Connecticut federal court. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine. BOP #03171-069.

cases.charges: Conspiracy and related federal charges — 1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation

name: Dora García-López
description: Chicago Puerto Rican community member prosecuted in the 1986 federal 'escape conspiracy' trial that alleged a plan to free imprisoned FALN members. The case was viewed by independence supporters as targeting the broader independence community in Chicago. Documented in PFOC Breakthrough magazine.

cases.charges: Conspiracy — 1986 Chicago 'escape conspiracy' trial
